title: Labour Rights — Art 19(1)(c)
lead: In 1921, a Madras court fined ==B. P. Wadia== and his fellow organisers £2,000 for the ‘conspiracy’ of forming a trade union. The Constitution guarantees that very act as a fundamental right — yet in 2026, an estimated ==7–8 million gig and platform workers== have no recognised forum in which to exercise it.

blocks.text: Indian workers have an unimpeachable ==right to unionise==, but no co-extensive constitutional right to be ==bargained with, to strike, or to picket== effectively. Those entitlements exist only as ==statutory creations== — and statutes can be narrowed by ordinary legislative majority in a way the Constitution cannot.

items: Early labour movement: ==Madras Labour Union (1918)== by B. P. Wadia — unions initially treated as ==criminal conspiracies== under colonial law
==Trade Unions Act, 1926==: legal recognition and immunity from certain civil/criminal liabilities
Nov 2025: four ==Labour Codes== brought into force — Wages (2019), Industrial Relations (2020), Social Security (2020), OSH (2020) — consolidating ==29 central statutes== including the 1926 Act

head: Case
Ruling
rows: Struck down a law banning an association without judicial review — ==‘reasonableness’ under 19(4) must be tested objectively by courts==, not left to executive satisfaction.
Right to form unions ==does not guarantee every union objective== (e.g. compulsory recognition); collective bargaining may be regulated by ordinary law.
Compulsorily altering a voluntary association's composition violates 19(1)(c) — the right includes associating ==only with those voluntarily admitted==.
Upheld restrictions for armed-forces-attached civilian staff under ==Art 33== — special carve-outs narrow 19(1)(c).
Strike recognised as an ==‘important weapon’== of trade unions — but not elevated to fundamental-right status; a tension never fully resolved.
Government employees have ==no fundamental or even statutory right to strike==.

items.title: Changes That Strengthen Workers
bullets: ==Sole Negotiating Union recalibrated==: IR Code's 51% threshold lowered to ==30% membership by secret ballot== (IR (Central) Rules, 2026), recognition valid three years

==Gig and platform workers named in statute for the first time== — Social Security Code mandates aggregator contributions (==1–2% of turnover==) to a Social Security Fund; Aadhaar-linked worker registration
Universal ==Grievance Redressal Committees== and a ==Re-skilling Fund== (15 days' wages per retrenched worker) mandatory regardless of union presence

items.title: Changes That Weaken Protection
bullets: ==Decriminalisation of compliance failures== — fines ₹50,000–₹10 lakh replace imprisonment; weaker deterrent against unfair labour practices
==Retrenchment/closure threshold raised from 100 to 300 workers== — most mid-sized factories can now lay off without prior government permission
Strike conditions stringent: ==60-day notice==, bans during conciliation/tribunal proceedings
==No uniform minimum wages== under the notified rules — state-wise fluctuations

items: ==Gap between law and practice==: legal recognition, weak effective protection.
==Post-1991 flexibility shift==: labour policy prioritises growth; IR Code subsumes the Trade Unions Act with minimal substantive change.
==Colonial continuities==: restrictive provisions echo the Public Safety Bill (1928) and Trade Disputes Act (1929) — continuing state control over labour mobilisation.
==Exclusion of informal & platform workers==: ‘gig’ and ‘platform’ absent from the IR Code — workers classed as ==independent contractors==, denied collective bargaining and union protections.

==Federal unevenness (mid-2026)==: Gujarat and Arunachal have notified rules under all four codes; West Bengal under none — a worker's protection ==depends on their state== (labour is on the Concurrent List).

items.title: Zomato Fleets (2024)
bullets: March 2024 plan for separate ==veg/non-veg fleets with distinct uniforms== risked exposing riders' religious and caste identity to discrimination.

items.title: TGPWU & State Laws
bullets: Telangana Gig and Platform Workers Union helped draft the ==Telangana Gig Workers Bill, 2025== — following ==Rajasthan (2023)== and ==Karnataka (2025)==.

items.title: IFAT Petition (2021)
bullets: Indian Federation of App-based Transport Workers (~==35,000 members==) petitioned that treating gig workers as ==‘partners’ rather than employees== violates Arts 14, 21 and 23.

items: ==Lower the bargaining threshold further== — and extend it to platforms
==Standardise state rule-making== on a fixed timeline
Build a transparent ==grievance-to-tribunal pipeline== for platform disputes

blocks.text: The deepest lesson of this history is that Article 19(1)(c) was never meant to be self-executing. It is a constitutional promise that requires continuous, deliberate statutory maintenance to remain real.
